 =E2=80=9Cunconventional.=E2=80=9D In a brief conversation on Wednesday, sh=

Post Haste

Across the Potomac, Washington Post staff continue to wait with bated breat=
h for Will Lewis to appoint the paper=E2=80=99s next executive editor, a de=
cision he is likely to announce before the end of the month. Both New York =
Times deputy editor Cliff Levy and Post acting executive editor Matt Murray=
 are still in the running=E2=80=94though Oliver Darcy reported recently tha=
t Murray is beginning to acknowledge that he isn=E2=80=99t going to get the=
 job. Somewhat amusingly, a rumor went flying around the Post earlier this =
week that former Times opinion editor James Bennet was under consideration,=
 which is not the case. All this speculation, the lack of a compelling and =
innovative candidate, and the oft-extended timeline suggests that either Je=
ff Bezos is too busy to meet the finalists
or, more likely, Lewis and his boss are still rubbing their temples. Levy w=
ants the job, but if they were going to make him an offer, wouldn=E2=80=99t=
 they have done so already?

In any event, it will not be veteran managing editor Matea Gold, who on Mon=
day opted to end her 12-year run at the paper and join The New York Times, =
where she will serve as Washington editor. Will wants a change agent, and M=
atea had always been a long shot for the position by virtue of being a vete=
ran, as I noted ( https://puck.news/msnbc-exit-strategies-washington-post-n=
ew-executive-editor/?utm_campaign=3DIn+the+Room+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%2812%2F11%2F=
24%29&utm_content=3DIn+The+Room+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%2812%2F11%2F24%29&utm_medium=
=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ) la=
st week. Indeed, the fact that she was even under consideration may have si=
mply been an attempt to placate her fellow Post veterans. Either way, Matea=
 never got to
meet with Bezos, who has historically held in-person interviews with execut=
ive editor candidates.

To that end, Matea=E2=80=99s departure to the Post=E2=80=99s longtime rival=
 was yet another blow to morale for the already dispirited newsroom=E2=80=
=94=E2=80=9Cdevastating,=E2=80=9D per Post columnist Karen Tumulty,=E2=80=
=9D and =E2=80=9Ca profound loss,=E2=80=9D according to national editor Phi=
l Rucker. Meanwhile, many other Post journalists now seem to be loose in th=
e saddle and considering their options. When asked if there were any indica=
tions of whom the next editor might be, one senior staffer replied only, =
=E2=80=9CNo idea. Everyone is in a state of grief.=E2=80=9D

Grief is the operative word here. The Post, after all, has been in a largel=
y uninterrupted fugue state since the late spring when, you know, Lewis def=
enestrated Sally Buzbee, lost his top choice for editor to an internal rebe=
llion, and nearly saw his own epitaph written in the
process. As they wait and mourn, however, the Posties would be wise to cue =
up ( https://youtu.be/s71nJQqzYRQ?feature=3Dshared&utm_campaign=3DIn+the+Ro=
om+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%2812%2F11%2F24%29&utm_content=3DIn+The+Room+-+SUBSCRIBERS=
+%2812%2F11%2F24%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_=
term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ) Andrew Ross Sorkin=E2=80=99s hourlong interview=
 with Bezos from last week=E2=80=99s DealBook Summit. During their chat, Be=
zos acknowledged that he had more than a few ideas about how to fix his bel=
oved paper.

Then, the conversation inevitably turned to Bezos=E2=80=99s other lines of =
business=E2=80=94chairing the $2 trillion-plus market cap Amazon and coloni=
zing the galaxy via Blue Origin, among them. Post insiders may want to ask =
themselves: When Bezos hurtles through the solar system, envisioning the fu=
ture of his business interests and mankind, is Cliff Levy the face of innov=
ation he sees on the moon?

x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">On Wednesday mornin=
g, about an hour or so after I broke the <u><a href=3D"https://email.puck.n=
ews/e/c/eyJlbWFpbF9pZCI6ImRnVDJ4Z1lEQUt2UktxclJLZ0dUdUdGUDVRRER3dHBKVktVby0=
zTT0iLCJocmVmIjoiaHR0cHM6Ly94LmNvbS9EeWxhbkJ5ZXJzL3N0YXR1cy8xODY2ODYxODQ5NT=
IzOTI5MzA0P3V0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1Jbit0aGUrUm9vbSstK1NVQlNDUklCRVJTKyUyODEyJTJGM=
TElMkYyNCUyOVx1MDAyNnV0bV9jb250ZW50PUluK1RoZStSb29tKy0rU1VCU0NSSUJFUlMrJTI4=
MTIlMkYxMSUyRjI0JTI5XHUwMDI2dXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbF9hY3Rpb25cdTAwMjZ1dG1fc29=
1cmNlPWN1c3RvbWVyLmlvXHUwMDI2dXRtX3Rlcm09ZjZjNjA2MTdkYTQyYWJkMTJhIiwiaW50ZX=
JuYWwiOiJmNmM2MDYxN2RhNDJhYmQxMmEiLCJsaW5rX2lkIjoyMDE3NTI2fQ/0d66d2791c4de5=
4b8b10cd81f9d2cd3b63c3a5e8180c85298c4b511cad32f694" style=3D"color:#d40000;=
text-decoration:none;font-weight:bold" class=3D"sdf">news</a></u>, Politico=
 co-founder and top editor <b>John Harris </b>announced that the site had h=
ired <b>Dasha Burns</b>, the 32-year-old NBC News political correspondent, =
to serve as White House bureau chief. On one level, the hire seemed like an=
other garden-variety, post-election trade window acquisition, albeit dresse=
d up with typical, avuncular Harrisian hyperbole: Politico had been in =E2=
=80=9Curgent and lively conversations with dozens of talented journalists,=
=E2=80=9D the elder statesman wrote. =E2=80=9CWe are organizing for impact,=
=E2=80=9D he declared. And then there was=E2=80=94wait for
it=E2=80=94that old Politico internal memo standby: =E2=80=9CIn many ways, =
Dasha was born for this moment.=E2=80=9D Generals have sent soldiers to the=
ir deaths with less.<br/><br/>In any event, Dasha=E2=80=99s move is indicat=
ive of the lame-duck season recalibration of the political media industry a=
t large. By Dasha=E2=80=99s own admission, the move from rising broadcast s=
tar to digital bureau chief is =E2=80=9Cunconventional.=E2=80=9D In a brief=
 conversation on Wednesday, she acknowledged that she was =E2=80=9Cnot a D.=
C. cat,=E2=80=9D had previously been averse to moving from Brooklyn to Wash=
ington, and that she had not been anticipating this kind of role after year=
s on various campaign trails. She might have ended up in Washington either =
way: The initial post-<b>Biden</b> job offer from NBC News, I=E2=80=99m tol=
d, was as a congressional reporter. Instead, she opted to =E2=80=9Csee the =
[<b>Trump</b>] story through=E2=80=9D at the White House, and to do so with=
 a more august title for an organization she said was =E2=80=9Con the foref=
ront of what
journalism can look like.=E2=80=9D (She and Harris are peas in a pod=E2=80=
=A6)<br/><br/>Dasha, largely an unknown in the industry, was certainly a ri=
sing star inside NBC News, and several people at 30 Rock and 400 North Cap =
texted me on Wednesday to bemoan her departure, which they attributed to ba=
d management. To her credit, Dasha may have been young enough to understand=
 that television is no longer the be-all and end-all of political journalis=
m. =E2=80=9CIt=E2=80=99s what I grew up in, but I want to be as forward-loo=
king as possible,=E2=80=9D she told me. =E2=80=9CI think it=E2=80=99s now m=
ore important than ever for individual journalists to be able to flex all o=
f those separate muscles. I can write for digital, I can do audio, I can do=
 television. I want to be ready for the future.=E2=80=9D (Again, the Harris=
 gene=E2=80=A6)<br/><br/>Inarguably, it=E2=80=99s never been easier for a y=
oung TV journalist to make that jump. The 2024 election, which news executi=
ves had hoped would reinvigorate anemic ratings, ultimately served as a
punctuation mark on the familiar story of the industry=E2=80=99s decline. T=
he days since then have been especially dispiriting: MSNBC, the primary the=
ater for Dasha=E2=80=99s reporting, has lost <i>half </i>of its audience si=
nce November 5, and is now watched by an average of fewer than half a milli=
on people, and just <i>43,000 </i>people below the age of 55. As the busine=
ss gets smaller, the earnings potential for sub-marquee talent across the c=
able landscape shrinks with it. Furthermore, MSNBC=E2=80=99s possible futur=
e as a liberal opinion pure play=E2=80=94post Comcast spinoff=E2=80=94could=
 have further restricted her real estate to NBC News, where she=E2=80=99d h=
ave to fight for the occasional three-minute package on <i>Today </i>or <i>=
Nightly News</i> against late-middle-age, sharp-elbowed, grinfucking surviv=
ors, all of whom speak the lingua franca of teamwork by day while fearfully=
 texting their agents after the second glass of wine each night. So, why no=
t take a chance on a bigger role at a digital
outfit?<br/></td>

x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">Across the Potomac,=
 <i>Washington Post</i> staff continue to wait with bated breath for <b>Wil=

n he is likely to announce before the end of the month. Both <i>New York Ti=
mes </i>deputy editor <b>Cliff Levy </b>and <i>Post </i>acting executive ed=
itor <b>Matt Murray </b>are still in the running=E2=80=94though <b>Oliver D=
arcy </b>reported recently that Murray is beginning to acknowledge that he =
isn=E2=80=99t going to get the job. Somewhat amusingly, a rumor went flying=
 around the <i>Post </i>earlier this week that former <i>Times </i>opinion =
editor <b>James Bennet </b>was under consideration, which is <i>not </i>the=
 case. All this speculation, the lack of a compelling and innovative candid=
ate, and the oft-extended timeline suggests that either <b>Jeff Bezos </b>i=
s too busy to meet the finalists
or, more likely, Lewis and his boss are still rubbing their temples. Levy w=
ants the job, but if they were going to make him an offer, wouldn=E2=80=99t=
 they have done so already?<br/><br/>In any event, it will <i>not </i>be ve=
teran managing editor <b>Matea Gold</b>, who on Monday opted to end her 12-=
year run at the paper and join <i>The New York Times</i>, where she will se=
rve as Washington editor. Will wants a change agent, and Matea had always b=
een a long shot for the position by virtue of being a veteran, as I <u><a h=

=9CNo idea. Everyone is in a state of grief.=E2=80=9D<br/><br/><i>Grief </i=
